design_philosophy.core_loop: Select a sport event, execute controller input patterns to simulate athletic movements, compete for high scores across diverse international sporting disciplines
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Learning and executing specific joystick patterns for each sport; precision timing and input consistency versus character performance
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Competing as an athlete in exotic international sports (sumo wrestling, cliff diving, caber toss, bull riding) without geographic constraints
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Treats sports simulation as abstract button-pattern games rather than realistic physics engines. Each event has 'bizarre little joystick patterns which have little to do with the events' themselves, prioritizing arcade accessibility over authenticity. Epyx's design philosophy emphasizes variety and consistent execution quality over thematic coherence.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Players choose competition mode (all events sequentially, selected events, single event, or practice mode), allowing self-directed difficulty scaling and practice opportunities

progression.unlock_mechanism: Events available for selection; player-directed event choice rather than narrative unlock
replayability_hooks: Multiple sport events with distinct mechanics prevent repetition
Practice mode for skill development on individual events
High score competition across different sports
Varied event difficulty creates natural progression within player-chosen order

world_building_techniques: Geographic location identification for each sport (Soviet Union weightlifting, France slalom skiing, Canada log rolling, Mexico cliff diving, Scotland caber toss, Germany barrel jumping, Japan sumo wrestling, United States bull riding)
Cultural sport authenticity through event selection and naming conventions

danger_signals: Bull riding event involves animal interaction with high-risk mechanics
environmental_storytelling: Water environments for log rolling and cliff diving
Sumo wrestling ring for Japanese competition
Arena settings for weightlifting and barrel jumping

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - World Games (NES)
